name: Stuart Russell
notes: Stuart Russell is a professor of computer science at the University of California, Berkeley and holds the Smith-Zadeh Chair in Engineering. He co-authored the textbook 'Artificial Intelligence: A Modern Approach' with Peter Norvig, which is used in over 1,500 universities in 135 countries. Russell founded the Center for Human-Compatible Artificial Intelligence at UC Berkeley in 2016 and serves as its director. He is president of the International Association for Safe and Ethical AI, which he co-founded.

name: Chuck Schumer
notes: Chuck Schumer is a Democratic U.S. Senator from New York who has served since 1999 and is currently Senate Majority Leader. He led a bipartisan working group that released a 31-page AI policy roadmap in May 2024, recommending at least $32 billion in government spending to accelerate AI research and development. The roadmap was developed after months of AI Insight Forums with tech companies, civil rights leaders, and other stakeholders. Schumer has described regulating artificial intelligence as "a challenge for Congress unlike any other" and has made election protection from AI interference a high priority.

name: Marc Andreessen
notes: Co-founder of Netscape (Mosaic browser) and co-founder/general partner of Andreessen Horowitz (a16z), the venture capital firm managing ~$42 billion in assets. In October 2023 published the 'Techno-Optimist Manifesto,' a 5,200-word essay arguing growth and unconstrained technological development are moral imperatives and that 'any deceleration of AI will cost lives... a form of murder.' Aligned with effective accelerationism (e/acc). Led a16z's high-profile 2024 lobbying campaign against California SB 1047 (the frontier-model safety bill authored by Sen. Scott Wiener), launching stopsb1047.com, hiring a Newsom advisor as lobbyist, and coordinating opposition with portfolio founders and Y Combinator; bill author Wiener said 'a16z was at the heart of a lot of the opposition.' In November 2024 joined with Microsoft on a joint plea against AI regulation. Net worth estimated ~$1.9 billion (Jan 2025).

name: Casey Newton
notes: Casey Newton is the founder and editor of Platformer, an independent publication devoted to exploring the intersection of technology and democracy. He co-hosts Hard Fork, a podcast about the future that is already here, alongside a colleague at The New York Times. Prior to founding Platformer in 2020, Casey spent 10 years covering Silicon Valley for The Verge, CNET, and the San Francisco Chronicle. In 2020, Casey was a finalist for the National Magazine Award for his series of investigative reports about content moderation that lead to a $52 million settlement for Facebook moderators who developed PTSD on the job.

name: Dario
notes: This is a test to add more information to the Dario entry and see how a second submission works with the UI

name: Brian Schatz
notes: Brian Schatz is Hawaii's senior United States Senator who has served since 2012 and focuses on helping workers, veterans, and families while leading key legislation on health care, climate change, and technology. He serves as Chairman of the Senate Committee on Indian Affairs and is a member of multiple committees including Appropriations, Commerce Science and Transportation, Foreign Relations, and the Select Committee on Ethics. Schatz is currently a candidate for Democratic whip, the No. 2 post in Senate Democratic leadership, after Dick Durbin announced he won't seek reelection. In 2024, as Chairman of Indian Affairs, he led passage of 16 bills and helped secure more than $15 billion in investments for Native communities nationwide.

name: Dario Amodei
notes: Dario Amodei (born 1983) is an American artificial intelligence researcher and entrepreneur who co-founded Anthropic in 2021 with his sister Daniela Amodei. He previously served as vice president of research at OpenAI, where he led the development of large language models like GPT-2 and GPT-3. Amodei earned his doctorate degree in biophysics from Princeton University as a Hertz Fellow, and was a postdoctoral scholar at the Stanford University School of Medicine. In 2025, Time magazine listed Amodei as one of the world's 100 most influential people.

name: Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez
notes: Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez (D-N.Y.) announced the Artificial Intelligence (AI) Data Center Moratorium Act on March 25, 2026, alongside Sen. Bernie Sanders. She announced on Friday that she is jumping into the race to be the top Democrat on the influential House Oversight Committee. Ocasio-Cortez was born on October 13, 1989, in the Bronx, New York, and began representing New York's 14th congressional district in 2019. Taking office at age 29, she became the youngest woman ever elected to Congress and is a member of the Democratic Party.

name: Marsha Blackburn
notes: Marsha Blackburn is the senior senator for Tennessee and the first woman to represent the Volunteer State in the United States Senate. She released a discussion draft of her legislative framework to codify President Trump's executive order to create one rulebook for artificial intelligence (AI) that protects children, creators, conservatives, and communities from harm while ensuring the United States wins the global race for AI supremacy. She was sworn into the 119th Congress to begin her second term in the United States Senate, taking the oath of office administered by U.S. Supreme Court Justice Amy Coney Barrett. She serves on the Deputy Whip Team and is a member of the Finance Committee; the Commerce, Science & Transportation Committee; the Veterans' Affairs Committee; and the Judiciary Committee.

name: Anton Korinek
notes: Anton Korinek is a Professor at the University of Virginia, Department of Economics and Darden School of Business as well as the Faculty Director of the Economics of Transformative AI (EconTAI) Initiative. He was named to the 2025 TIME100 AI list of the most influential people in artificial intelligence. He is a Nonresident Senior Fellow at Brookings and the Peterson Institute, a Research Associate at the NBER, a Research Fellow at the CEPR, and serves on Anthropic's Economic Advisory Council. He received his PhD from Columbia University in 2007 after several years of work experience in the IT and financial sectors.

name: Daniela Amodei
notes: Daniela Amodei is the president and co-founder of Anthropic, an AI safety and research company that develops the Claude large language model. She co-founded the company with her brother Dario Amodei in 2021 after leaving OpenAI, where she served as VP of Safety and Policy. According to Wikipedia, she was born in 1987 and graduated from the University of California, Santa Cruz with a Bachelor of Arts in English Literature. As of February 2026, Forbes estimated her net worth to be $7 billion.

name: David Evan Harris
notes: David Evan Harris is a Chancellor's Public Scholar at UC Berkeley and a continuing lecturer at the Haas School of Business. He was named one of Business Insider's top 100 people in AI in 2023. In his previous role at Meta (formerly Facebook), Harris managed teams of quantitative and qualitative researchers working on responsible AI, social impact, and civic integrity. He took a lead role in drafting California's AI Transparency Act of 2025 (AB 853).

name: Kim Stanley Robinson
notes: Kim Stanley Robinson is described as "one of human civilization's greatest living science fiction writers" and author of works including the Hugo Award-winning Mars trilogy, Nebula Award-winning 2312, and The Ministry for the Future. He was born on March 23, 1952, and is best known for his Mars trilogy of novels. Robinson earned a PhD in English from UC San Diego in 1982, writing his dissertation on Philip K. Dick under Marxist scholar Fredric Jameson. He has lived in Davis, California since 1978 and identifies as a democratic socialist, being a dues-paying member of the Democratic Socialists of America.

name: Jakub Pachocki
notes: Jakub Pachocki is OpenAI's chief scientist who sets the company's long-term research goals and played key roles in the development of both GPT-4 and reasoning models. He was born in 1991 in Gdańsk, Poland, and joined OpenAI in 2017, becoming chief scientist in May 2024 after Ilya Sutskever left the company. Pachocki obtained his PhD from Carnegie Mellon University under the supervision of Gary Miller and was a six-time finalist of the International Olympiad in Informatics, winning a silver medal in 2009. OpenAI CEO Sam Altman has called Pachocki 'easily one of the greatest minds of our generation'.

name: Jared Polis
notes: Jared Polis is the 43rd Governor of Colorado, having assumed office in 2019. He previously served as the United States Representative from Colorado's 2nd congressional district from 2009 to 2019. Polis convened the Colorado AI Policy Work Group to develop a framework addressing the use of AI and Automated Decision Making Systems in consequential decisions about consumers. He is the first openly gay man elected as governor in U.S. history.

name: Sam Altman
notes: Sam Altman is the CEO of OpenAI, the artificial intelligence research organization that developed ChatGPT. He became CEO of OpenAI in 2019 after co-founding the organization in 2015. In November 2023, he was briefly fired by OpenAI's board of directors but was reinstated five days later. His net worth was estimated at US$3.3 billion by Forbes in March 2026.

name: Mike Krieger
notes: Mike Krieger is the chief product officer of Anthropic. He co-founded Instagram with Kevin Systrom in 2010 and served as its Chief Technology Officer until 2018. He recently moved from his CPO role to co-lead Anthropic's internal incubator called the 'Labs' team. He previously co-founded the AI-powered news app Artifact which was sold to Yahoo.

name: Josh Woodward
notes: Josh Woodward is a 42-year-old Oklahoma native who started at Google by way of a product marketing internship in 2009. He is currently Vice President at Google, leading both Google Labs and the Gemini app. Woodward was promoted to run the Gemini app in April while keeping his existing job running Google Labs. Google said in October that the Gemini app's monthly active users swelled to 650 million from 350 million in March.

name: Lawrence Lessig
notes: Lawrence Lessig is the Roy L. Furman Professor of Law and Leadership at Harvard Law School. He is the founder of Equal Citizens and a founding board member of Creative Commons. He clerked for Judge Richard Posner on the 7th Circuit Court of Appeals and Justice Antonin Scalia on the United States Supreme Court. He has focused his work on campaign finance reform and institutional corruption, particularly regarding SuperPACs and their impact on democracy.

name: Eliezer Yudkowsky
notes: Eliezer Yudkowsky is an American artificial intelligence researcher and writer on decision theory and ethics, known for popularizing ideas related to friendly artificial intelligence. He is the founder of and a research fellow at the Machine Intelligence Research Institute (MIRI), a private research nonprofit based in Berkeley, California. He co-authored the New York Times Best Seller 'If Anyone Builds It, Everyone Dies: Why Superhuman AI Would Kill Us All' with Nate Soares. He founded LessWrong, a 'community blog devoted to refining the art of human rationality' in February 2009.

name: Michel Justen
notes: Michel Justen works as AI Grants Lead at Tarbell Center for AI Journalism, which is funded by Coefficient Giving (formerly Open Philanthropy). He co-founded EA UW in Fall 2020 alongside his twin Lenni and graduated from UW-Madison in 2022 with degrees in Neurobiology and Psychology. He previously worked as an AI Governance Fellow at Pivotal Research and helped run events at the Centre for Effective Altruism. Open Philanthropy recommended a grant of $13,000 to Michel Justen to support him as he explores career options that could be highly impactful.

name: Cory Booker
notes: Cory Booker is a U.S. Senator from New Jersey who was elected in 2013 and represents New Jersey as the state's first Black senator. He previously served as mayor of Newark from 2006 to 2013. In March 2025, Booker delivered the longest speech in Senate history, speaking for more than 25 hours in protest of the Trump administration's policies. He has been a leader in criminal justice reform and helped craft the First Step Act, which became law in December 2018.

name: Kyle Fish
notes: Kyle Fish is Anthropic's first in-house AI welfare researcher, hired to determine whether models like Claude can have conscious experiences and how the company should respond. He is trained in neuroscience and spent years in biotech, cofounding companies that used machine learning to design drugs and vaccines for pandemic preparedness. Fish estimates a 20% chance that today's large language models have some form of conscious experience, though he stresses that consciousness should be seen as a spectrum, not binary. He co-authored a paper titled "Taking AI Welfare Seriously" with collaborators including David Chalmers and others.

name: Simon Johnson
notes: Simon Johnson is the Ronald A. Kurtz (1954) Professor of Entrepreneurship at the MIT Sloan School of Management and co-winner of the 2024 Nobel Prize in Economic Sciences jointly with Daron Acemoglu and James A. Robinson. He co-directs the Shaping the Future of Work initiative at MIT, focusing on the economics and politics of AI development and deployment. Johnson served as Chief Economist of the International Monetary Fund from March 2007 through August 2008. His research examines how institutions are formed and affect prosperity, and he advocates for developing "Pro-Worker AI" technologies that can increase productivity and wages for less-educated workers.

name: Michael Kratsios
notes: Michael Kratsios serves as Assistant to the President and Director of the White House Office of Science and Technology Policy, confirmed by the Senate in a 74-25 vote on March 25, 2025. He previously served as the fourth Chief Technology Officer of the United States during President Trump's first term and as Under Secretary of War from January 2020 to January 2021. Prior to government service, he was a Managing Director at Scale AI from January 2021 to January 2025 and held various positions at Thiel Capital LLC. He graduated from Princeton University with a B.A. in politics and served as a Visiting Scholar at Tsinghua University.

name: Jennifer Pahlka
notes: Jennifer Pahlka is the founder of Code for America and author of 'Recoding America: Why Government Is Failing in the Digital Age and How We Can Do Better'. She served as U.S. Deputy Chief Technology Officer under President Obama from 2013-2014, where she helped found the United States Digital Service. She is currently a senior fellow at the Niskanen Center and the Federation of American Scientists. In October 2025, she announced the launch of the Recoding America Fund, a pooled philanthropic effort that will raise and deploy at least $120 million over six years.

name: Yoshua Bengio
notes: Yoshua Bengio is a Canadian computer scientist born March 5, 1964, in Paris, France, and is a full professor at Université de Montréal. He received the 2018 A.M. Turing Award together with Geoffrey Hinton and Yann LeCun for their foundational work on deep learning. In June 2025, Bengio launched a nonprofit organization called LawZero aimed at building 'honest' AI systems that can detect and block harmful behavior by autonomous agents. He chairs the International AI Safety Report, an annual study that seeks to compile scientific evidence on emerging AI risks.

name: Paul Christiano
notes: Paul Christiano is Head of AI Safety for the U.S. Artificial Intelligence Safety Institute at NIST, where he designs and conducts tests of frontier AI models focusing on capabilities of national security concern. He founded the Alignment Research Center, a nonprofit research organization that seeks to align future machine learning systems with human interests. He previously ran the language model alignment team at OpenAI, where he pioneered work on reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF). He holds a Ph.D. in computer science from the University of California, Berkeley, and a B.S. in mathematics from the Massachusetts Institute of Technology.

name: Regina Barzilay
notes: Regina Barzilay is a School of Engineering Distinguished Professor of AI & Health in the Department of Computer Science and the AI Faculty Lead at MIT Jameel Clinic. She was diagnosed with breast cancer in 2014, which prompted her to conduct research in oncology. She develops machine learning methods for drug discovery and clinical AI, and her research has been recognized with the MacArthur Fellowship, an NSF Career Award, and the AAAI Squirrel AI Award for Artificial Intelligence for the Benefit of Humanity. Regina is a member of the National Academy of Engineering, American Academy of Arts and Sciences, and the National Academy of Medicine.

name: Cynthia Lummis
notes: Senator Cynthia Lummis (R-WY) introduced the Responsible Innovation and Safe Expertise (RISE) Act of 2025 on June 12, 2025, clarifying liability frameworks for AI used by professionals. She announced on December 19, 2025, that she will not seek re-election to the U.S. Senate in 2026, stating "I do not have six more years in me". Lummis is a major crypto supporter and the leading advocate for creating a Strategic Bitcoin Reserve. She was sworn into the United States Senate on January 3, 2021, becoming the first woman to serve as United States Senator from Wyoming.

name: Demis Hassabis
notes: Demis Hassabis is the CEO and co-founder of Google DeepMind, an artificial intelligence company that was founded in London in 2010 and acquired by Google in 2014. He shared the 2024 Nobel Prize in Chemistry with John M. Jumper for their work on AlphaFold, an AI tool that predicts protein structures. He is also the founder and CEO of Isomorphic Labs, a biotechnology company focused on drug discovery. Hassabis was born on July 27, 1976, in London to a Greek Cypriot father and Chinese Singaporean mother.

name: Gina Raimondo
notes: Gina Raimondo served as the 40th U.S. Secretary of Commerce from March 3, 2021 to January 20, 2025 under President Biden. She previously served as the 75th Governor of Rhode Island from 2015 to 2021 and was the first woman to serve in that role. She co-founded Point Judith Capital, Rhode Island's first venture capital firm, in 2001. Since leaving government service, she became a Distinguished Fellow at the Council on Foreign Relations in January 2025 and has been considering a potential 2028 presidential run.

name: Timnit Gebru
notes: Timnit Gebru is the founder and executive director of The Distributed Artificial Intelligence Research (DAIR) Institute. She was co-lead of the Ethical AI research team at Google until her firing in 2020 (along with her co-lead) for writing a paper warning about the dangers of large language models and raising issues of discrimination in the workplace. She co-founded Black in AI, a nonprofit that works to increase the presence, inclusion, visibility and health of Black people in the field of AI. She is the recipient of the 2025 Miles Conrad Award, NISO's lifetime achievement award for those working in the information community.

name: Fran Drescher
notes: Fran Drescher is the SAG-AFTRA President who led the union during a 118-day strike and warned about AI threats to the entertainment industry. She became one of the prominent faces of the strike alongside WGA West President Meredith Stiehm and WGA East President Lisa Takeuchi Cullen. Drescher took office as president on October 15, 2021, and led the union during the five-month actors' strike that began on July 14, 2023. She has been vocal about protecting actors from AI technology and securing proper compensation and consent for use of their likeness.

name: Ro Khanna
notes: Ro Khanna is a U.S. Representative from California's 17th Congressional District, representing Silicon Valley since 2017. He serves as Ranking Member of the House Select Committee on China and is a member of committees including Judiciary, Transportation & Infrastructure. Khanna authored the Epstein Files Transparency Act and led a bipartisan resolution demanding the DOJ to release the Epstein files. He has proposed seven principles for democratic AI regulation and advocates for stronger government oversight of artificial intelligence.

name: Peter Thiel
notes: Peter Thiel is the billionaire co-founder of PayPal and chairman of Palantir. He argues that artificial intelligence is targeting 'math people' - coders, engineers, quants, data scientists, and algorithmic specialists - rather than creative writers, artists, or storytellers first. In a 2019 talk, Thiel suggested AI itself might be inherently authoritarian, calling AI 'communist' and warning it could bring back a world of 'a few large companies, a few large governments, a few large computers that controlled everything'. He has been giving lectures on the Antichrist, first in San Francisco in September 2025, then in Paris in January, and most recently in Rome, where his Antichrist is described as 'a Luddite who wants to stop all science'.

name: Saikat Chakrabarti
notes: Saikat Chakrabarti is running for election to the U.S. House to represent California's 11th Congressional District in the primary scheduled for June 2, 2026. He was born in Fort Worth, Texas and earned a bachelor's degree from Harvard University in 2007. His career experience includes being a co-founder of New Consensus and Justice Democrats, chief of staff for Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez, organizing technology for Bernie Sanders' campaign, and second engineer at Stripe. He is advocating for a four-day workweek in response to AI productivity gains, arguing that companies like Meta are 'squeezing' workers with increased productivity standards without additional compensation.

name: Pete Buttigieg
notes: Pete Buttigieg served as U.S. Secretary of Transportation from 2021 to 2025 under President Joe Biden. He was the first openly gay Cabinet secretary in U.S. history and the youngest person ever to serve as Secretary of Transportation. He previously served as mayor of South Bend, Indiana from 2012 to 2020. He is currently a Winter-Spring 2025 Pritzker Fellow at the University of Chicago Institute of Politics.

name: Chris Murphy
notes: Chris Murphy is the junior United States Senator from Connecticut, serving since 2013. He previously served in the U.S. House of Representatives from 2007 to 2013, representing Connecticut's 5th congressional district. Murphy was reelected to a third Senate term in 2024, defeating Republican challenger Matthew Corey. He has become a prominent voice on AI regulation, arguing that unregulated artificial intelligence will cause massive job displacement and economic collapse.

name: David Sacks
notes: David Sacks stepped down from his role as White House AI and crypto czar in March 2026, having reached the end of his 130-day limit as a special government employee. He is now co-chair of the President's Council of Advisors on Science and Technology alongside Michael Kratsios. Sacks is a general partner of Craft Ventures, a venture capital fund he co-founded in late 2017. He was previously COO and product leader of PayPal and is considered a member of the 'PayPal Mafia'.

name: Tom Kalil
notes: Tom Kalil is the CEO of Renaissance Philanthropy, which he launched in May 2024. He served in the White House for two presidents (Obama and Clinton) and worked with the Senate to give every federal agency the authority to support incentive prizes for up to $50 million. He designed and launched dozens of White House science and technology initiatives, including the National Nanotechnology Initiative (announced by President Clinton in January 2000 and launched at ~$500M/year in FY2001) and The BRAIN Initiative announced by President Obama. Prior to launching Renaissance Philanthropy, he was Chief Innovation Officer at Schmidt Futures.

name: Ben Reinhardt
notes: Ben Reinhardt is the founder of Speculative Technologies, a nonprofit research organization working to turn science fiction into reality. He previously worked as an Entrepreneur-in-Residence at Entrepreneur First in Singapore, where he helped people build deep tech companies from scratch. His background includes work at NASA, Magic Leap, venture capital, and starting a robotics company.

name: Bruce Reed
notes: Bruce Reed is an American political advisor and policy strategist who served as deputy chief of staff for policy in the Biden administration and led government-wide AI policy efforts. He was dubbed Biden's "AI Whisperer" by Politico and led efforts that produced voluntary commitments on AI safety from leading tech companies and a sweeping Executive Order on safe and responsible AI. In March 2025, he was appointed as Head of Common Sense AI at Common Sense Media to champion AI safety for children and families. He previously served as chief domestic policy advisor to President Clinton and chief of staff to Vice President Biden during the Obama administration.

name: Jeffrey Kessler
notes: Jeffrey Kessler is the Under Secretary for Industry and Security at the U.S. Commerce Department, where he leads the Bureau of Industry and Security (BIS). He testified before Congress in June 2025 that Huawei's AI chip production capacity for 2025 is estimated at "at or below 200,000" units. He announced that the Biden administration's AI Diffusion Rule would be rescinded, labeling it as "divorced from commercial reality". He was confirmed by the U.S. Senate in March 2025 as a Trump nominee and is a former partner at WilmerHale.

name: Mark Zuckerberg
notes: Founder, CEO and Chairman of Meta Platforms (parent of Facebook, Instagram, WhatsApp). Drove Meta's open-weights Llama strategy, positioning Llama models as the open-source alternative to closed frontier labs. After internal frustration with Llama 4's performance in mid-2025, Zuckerberg launched Meta Superintelligence Labs (MSL) in June 2025, restructured in August 2025 into four teams (TBD Lab developing Llama under chief AI officer Alexandr Wang, FAIR under Rob Fergus, plus product and infrastructure units), and pursued an aggressive 2025 hiring spree with reported signing bonuses up to $1 billion to recruit top AI talent. Meta's 2025 capex projected at ~$68 billion, the bulk allocated to AI. Zuckerberg frames the goal as 'personal superintelligence' — AI that empowers individual users — distinct from labs pursuing centralized automation of work. Reportedly building an internal AI agent to assist his own executive duties.

name: Elon Musk
notes: Elon Musk is a businessman and entrepreneur known for his leadership of Tesla, SpaceX, X, and xAI. Musk has been the wealthiest person in the world since 2025; as of April 2026, Forbes estimates his net worth to be US$809 billion. He made predictions at the World Economic Forum that artificial intelligence will be smarter than all of humanity by the end of the decade. Musk spent more than $290 million on the 2024 election, boosting President Donald Trump and Republicans.

name: Liang Wenfeng
notes: Liang Wenfeng is a Chinese entrepreneur born in 1985 who serves as CEO of DeepSeek and co-founder of quantitative hedge fund High-Flyer. He launched DeepSeek in 2023 with backing from High-Flyer, focusing on developing general artificial intelligence through efficient, low-cost approaches. DeepSeek gained international attention in January 2025 with the release of DeepSeek-R1, which achieved performance comparable to top models like GPT-4 at significantly lower training costs. He was ranked No. 5 on AI Magazine's Top 100 Leaders 2026 list and included in Nature's top 10 'people who shaped science in 2025'.

name: Jared Kaplan
notes: Jared Kaplan is Anthropic's chief scientist and co-founder. He previously worked as a researcher at OpenAI where he co-authored "Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models" (2020) and "Language Models are Few-Shot Learners" (2020), which introduced GPT-3. He is also an associate professor in the Johns Hopkins University Department of Physics & Astronomy. In October 2024, Anthropic announced that Kaplan would serve as the company's "Responsible Scaling Officer", overseeing its responsible scaling policy (RSP).

name: Joanne Jang
notes: Joanne Jang is a key executive at OpenAI who served as Head of Model Behavior and was recognized on the TIME100 AI 2025 list. She announced her departure from OpenAI after four and a half years, having joined when it was a research lab with fewer than 200 employees. Her key contributions included work on GPT-4, DALL-E 2, Text-to-Speech, Chat API, and Memory features for ChatGPT. She is departing to establish OAI Labs, a new group focused on prototyping interfaces for human-AI collaboration.

name: Daron Acemoglu
notes: Daron Acemoglu is an Institute Professor at MIT who won the 2024 Nobel Prize in Economics with Simon Johnson and James A. Robinson for studies of how institutions are formed and affect prosperity. He argues that AI is not improving productivity as much as hyped, projecting only modest GDP gains of 1.1% to 1.6% over the next decade. Acemoglu believes current AI development focuses too much on automation rather than complementing human workers. He co-authored the book 'Power and Progress: Our Thousand-Year Struggle Over Technology and Prosperity' with Simon Johnson.

name: Ganesh Sitaraman
notes: Ganesh Sitaraman is the New York Alumni Chancellor's Chair in Law at Vanderbilt University and Director of the Program on Law and Government. He is a longtime advisor to Elizabeth Warren, serving in various capacities including as Policy Director during her 2012 Senate campaign and senior counsel in the Senate. He is the author of several books including 'The Crisis of the Middle-Class Constitution' and 'The Great Democracy'. In 2018, Sitaraman was awarded an Andrew Carnegie Fellowship.

name: Mariana Mazzucato
notes: Mariana Mazzucato is Professor in the Economics of Innovation and Public Value at University College London, where she is Founding Director of the UCL Institute for Innovation & Public Purpose. She is a member of the South African President's Economic Advisory Council since 2019, advising on areas such as green industrial strategy, state capacity, and reform of state-owned enterprises. In 2025, she was appointed Commander of the British Empire (CBE) for services to economics in the King's Birthday Honours List. President Cyril Ramaphosa has appointed her as Technical Expert to South Africa's G20 Presidency and his Special Presidential Representative to Taskforce 1: Inclusive Economic Growth, Industrialisation, Employment, and Reduced Inequality.

name: Fei-Fei Li
notes: Fei-Fei Li is a Chinese-born American computer scientist best known for establishing ImageNet, the dataset that enabled rapid advances in computer vision in the 2010s. She is a professor of computer science at Stanford University, with research expertise in artificial intelligence, machine learning, deep learning, computer vision, and cognitive neuroscience. She has been called the godmother of AI for her pioneering work in computer vision and image recognition. In 2024, she raised $230 million for a startup called World Labs, which she and three colleagues founded to develop a "spatial intelligence" AI technology.

name: Latanya Sweeney
notes: Latanya Sweeney is the Daniel Paul Professor of the Practice of Government and Technology at the Harvard Kennedy School and in the Harvard Faculty of Arts and Sciences. She was named to Time magazine's 2025 list of the 100 most influential people in the field of artificial intelligence. She is the founder and director of the Public Interest Tech Lab, founded in 2021 with a $3 million grant from the Ford Foundation. She earned her PhD in computer science from MIT in 2001, being the first Black woman to do so.

name: Sayash Kapoor
notes: Sayash Kapoor is a Ph.D. candidate in computer science at Princeton University's Center for Information Technology Policy. He is co-author with Arvind Narayanan of the book "AI Snake Oil: What Artificial Intelligence Can Do, What It Can't, and How to Tell the Difference". He was named among TIME's 100 most influential people in AI. He won the Porter Ogden Jacobus Fellowship, Princeton's top distinction for graduate students.

name: Sandy Pentland
notes: Alex Paul "Sandy" Pentland (born 1951) is an American computer scientist, HAI Fellow at Stanford, Toshiba Professor at MIT, and serial entrepreneur. He helped create and direct the MIT Media Lab and the Media Lab Asia in India, and is a HAI Fellow at Stanford. Pentland is one of the most cited authors in computer science, with an h-index of 155. He is a member of the U.S. National Academy of Engineering, co-led the World Economic Forum discussion in Davos that led to the EU privacy regulation GDPR, and was one of the UN Secretary General's "Data Revolutionaries" who helped forge the transparency and accountability mechanisms in the UN's Sustainable Development Goals.

name: Daniel Kokotajlo
notes: Daniel Kokotajlo is a former OpenAI researcher who resigned in 2024 and refused to sign a non-disparagement clause, costing him approximately $2 million in equity. He is the founder of the AI Futures Project and author of AI 2027, a detailed scenario showing how superintelligence might arrive by the end of the decade. His median forecast for AGI is 2029, and he believes the default trajectory could lead to AI takeover and human extinction unless there is international coordination. He was named to TIME's 100 Most Influential People in AI for both 2024 and 2025.

name: Marius Hobbhahn
notes: Marius Hobbhahn is the CEO and co-founder of Apollo Research, a nonprofit AI safety research organization that focuses on understanding AI deception and scheming behavior. In 2025, he was named to TIME100 in AI 2025. Apollo Research has collaborated with major AI companies including OpenAI to reduce scheming in models like o3, and works with government bodies like the U.K.'s AI Safety Institute. He holds a Ph.D. in Machine Learning from the University of Tübingen, where he worked under the supervision of Philipp Hennig.

name: Holden Karnofsky
notes: Holden Karnofsky cofounded both the charity evaluator GiveWell and the philanthropic foundation Open Philanthropy (now called Coefficient Giving), which has now directed over $4 billion in grants. He led Open Phil for seven years, during which he also served on the board of OpenAI for four years. Reflecting his growing focus on AGI, he recently joined the frontier AI company Anthropic, which his wife Daniela Amodei helped cofound back in 2021. Karnofsky joined Anthropic in January 2025 as member of the technical staff, working on Anthropic's responsible scaling policies and other safety planning aspects.

name: Ed Newton-Rex
notes: Ed Newton-Rex is the CEO of Fairly Trained, a non-profit he founded in 2024 that certifies generative AI companies for fairer training data practices. He previously founded Jukedeck in 2010, one of the first AI music generation startups, which was acquired by ByteDance in 2019. He was named in TIME Magazine's list of the 100 most influential people in artificial intelligence in 2025. He organized the Statement on AI Training, an open letter rejecting unfair generative AI training practices signed by more than 50,000 people, and 'Is This What We Want?', a protest album co-written by 1,000 UK musicians.

name: Joshua Kushner
notes: Joshua Kushner is the founder and managing partner of Thrive Capital, a venture capital firm. He co-founded Oscar Health, a health insurance startup that went public in 2021. Kushner's firm has been one of OpenAI's major investors, with Thrive Capital investing roughly $1 billion in OpenAI at a $285 billion valuation in December. He is married to supermodel Karlie Kloss and they have two sons together.

name: Duncan Crabtree-Ireland
notes: Duncan Crabtree-Ireland is the National Executive Director and Chief Negotiator of SAG-AFTRA, overseeing the world's largest entertainment union with more than 160,000 members worldwide. He was the lead negotiator during the 2023 SAG-AFTRA strike and has been a key advocate for AI protections in the entertainment industry. He previously served as SAG-AFTRA's Chief Operating Officer and General Counsel, and was a key participant in the successful merger between SAG and AFTRA. He co-hosts the SAG-AFTRA Podcast and SAG-AFTRA Podcast en Español.

name: Dan Wang
notes: There are multiple individuals named Dan Wang who are journalists or writers. One Dan Wang is a Canadian technology analyst and writer specializing in contemporary China, who has been an analyst at Gavekal Dragonomics and a research fellow at the Hoover Institution. Another Dan Wang is Lambert Family Professor of Social Enterprise at Columbia Business School and creator of CAiSEY, a voice-powered AI discussion partner. A third Dan Wang is a director on Eurasia Group's China team, previously serving as chief economist at Hang Seng Bank China.

name: Milagros Miceli
notes: Milagros Miceli is a sociologist and computer scientist who founded the Data Workers' Inquiry, an academic project that puts power in the hands of AI data labelers to publish research by and about themselves. She is based at the Weizenbaum Institute in Germany and was named to TIME's 2025 TIME100 AI list. Miceli is 41 years old and is described as an Argentine researcher who has lived in Berlin for more than 16 years. She does not use ChatGPT, stating it would feel like "a betrayal" because she works closely with people who worked on ChatGPT training and "it has destroyed their lives".

name: Ezra Klein
notes: Ezra Klein is a New York Times columnist, best-selling author, and host of The Ezra Klein Show podcast. He is a co-founder of Vox and was formerly the website's editor-at-large. He has written two books: Why We're Polarized in January 2020, and Abundance, cowritten with Derek Thompson, in March 2025. Klein is married to Annie Lowrey, who writes about economics at The Atlantic, and they have two children.

name: Mike Rounds
notes: Mike Rounds is a Republican U.S. Senator from South Dakota who was sworn into the United States Senate on January 6th, 2015. He serves as co-chair of the Senate AI Caucus and serves on five committees: Senate Appropriations; Armed Services; Banking, Housing and Urban Affairs; Indian Affairs; and the Select Committee on Intelligence. He previously served as the 31st governor of South Dakota from 2003 – 2011. He has been actively introducing bipartisan AI legislation, including the Economy of the Future Commission Act with Sen. Mark Warner in March 2026.

name: Dan Kagan-Kans
notes: Dan Kagan-Kans is described as a writer on AI, science, and ideas for publications like Transformer, the Wall Street Journal, and American Scholar. He was previously a program officer at the Tikvah Fund and lives in New York. He has written articles including "The Left Is Missing Out on AI" for Transformer and "Baby Shoggoth Is Listening" for The American Scholar.

name: Joi Ito
notes: Joichi 'Joi' Ito is a Japanese entrepreneur and venture capitalist who serves as president of Chiba Institute of Technology. He was director of the MIT Media Lab from 2011-2019, resigning in September 2019 following revelations about financial ties to Jeffrey Epstein. Ito founded multiple tech companies including PSINet Japan, Infoseek Japan, and Digital Garage, and was an early investor in Twitter, Kickstarter, and Flickr. He earned a PhD in Media and Governance from Keio University in 2018.

name: Chris Coons
notes: Chris Coons is a U.S. Senator from Delaware who serves on multiple committees including Appropriations, Foreign Relations, Judiciary, Small Business and Entrepreneurship, and Ethics committees. He is the Ranking Member of the Senate Foreign Relations Committee's Subcommittee on East Asia, The Pacific, and International Cybersecurity Policy and vice chair of the Ethics Committee. Coons has been actively involved in AI policy, introducing resolutions and legislation to maintain U.S. dominance in artificial intelligence and counter China's AI development efforts. He previously served as New Castle County Executive for six years and worked as Associate Counsel at W.L. Gore & Associates, Inc..

name: Martin Heinrich
notes: Martin Heinrich is a U.S. Senator from New Mexico who has served since January 2013. He is the founder and co-chair of the Senate Artificial Intelligence Caucus, which he established with former Senator Rob Portman (R-Ohio) in 2019. He serves as Ranking Member on the Committee on Energy and Natural Resources and serves on the Senate Appropriations, Intelligence, and Joint Economic Committees. Heinrich has a background in mechanical engineering and previously served two terms in the U.S. House of Representatives from 2009-2013.

name: Todd Young
notes: Todd Christopher Young is an American politician and attorney serving as the senior United States senator from Indiana, a seat he has held since 2017. He is a member of the Republican Party and previously served as the U.S. representative for Indiana's 9th congressional district. Young currently serves on the U.S. Senate Committees on Finance; Foreign Relations; Health, Education, Labor & Pensions; Commerce, Science & Transportation; and Small Business and Entrepreneurship. He is a member of the Bipartisan Senate AI Working Group along with Maj. Leader Chuck Schumer, Sen. Mike Rounds, and Sen. Martin Heinrich.

name: Ben Ray Luján
notes: Ben Ray Luján has represented New Mexico in the United States Senate since January 2021 and previously served as U.S. Representative for New Mexico's Third Congressional District and House Assistant Speaker in the 116th Congress. He sits on the Committee on Commerce, Science, and Transportation; the Committee on Finance; the Committee on Agriculture, Nutrition, and Forestry; the Committee on Indian Affairs; and the Committee on the Budget. Senator Luján has been particularly active in AI policy, introducing bipartisan legislation including the TEST AI Act of 2025 and the AI CONSENT Act to establish responsible guardrails for artificial intelligence systems. He grew up in Nambé, a small farming community north of Santa Fe, and earned his Bachelor's degree in Business Administration from New Mexico Highlands University.

name: Joni Ernst
notes: Joni Ernst is a U.S. Senator from Iowa who was first elected in 2014 and has served since January 6, 2015. She is a combat veteran who served as a company commander in Kuwait and Iraq during Operation Iraqi Freedom and retired as a Lieutenant Colonel in the Iowa Army National Guard after 23 years of military service. Ernst announced in September 2025 that she will not seek re-election to a third term in 2026. She serves as Chairman of the Senate Armed Services Emerging Threats and Capabilities Subcommittee and Chair of the Small Business & Entrepreneurship Committee.

name: Maggie Hassan
notes: Maggie Hassan is a U.S. Senator from New Hampshire serving since 2017, and previously served as the 81st Governor of New Hampshire from 2013 to 2017. She was named the most bipartisan member of Congress by the nonpartisan Common Ground Committee, becoming the first elected official to receive a perfect score on their scorecard. Hassan has recently introduced multiple AI-related bills, including the bipartisan AI Workforce PREPARE Act and the Reliable Artificial Intelligence Research Act of 2025. She serves as Ranking Member of the Joint Economic Committee and has been actively working on combating AI-enabled scams.

name: Jerry Moran
notes: Jerry Moran is a Republican U.S. Senator from Kansas who serves as a senior member of the Senate Committee on Commerce, Science and Transportation. He has worked extensively on bipartisan artificial intelligence legislation, introducing multiple AI-related bills in 2026 with Democratic Senator Maria Cantwell. Moran serves as Chairman on the Appropriations Subcommittee on Commerce, Justice, Science, and Related Agencies and as Chairman of the Senate Committee on Veterans' Affairs. He was first elected to the U.S. Senate in 2010 after serving seven terms in the U.S. House of Representatives representing Kansas's 1st congressional district.

name: John Hickenlooper
notes: John Hickenlooper is a U.S. Senator from Colorado who serves as Ranking Member of the Senate Commerce Committee's Subcommittee on Consumer Protection, Technology, and Data Privacy. He has been actively involved in AI policy, reintroducing the bipartisan VET AI Act with Senator Shelley Moore Capito to boost AI standards and create guidelines for third-party evaluations of AI systems. Hickenlooper previously served as Governor of Colorado from 2011 to 2019 and as Mayor of Denver from 2003 to 2011. He started his career as a geologist and later opened Colorado's first brewpub in downtown Denver.

name: Jon Husted
notes: Jon Husted is a Republican U.S. Senator from Ohio who began serving in January 2025 after being appointed to fill the vacancy created by JD Vance's resignation. He previously served as Ohio's 66th lieutenant governor from 2019 to 2025 and as Ohio's 50th secretary of state from 2011 to 2019. As lieutenant governor, he led regulatory reform efforts using artificial intelligence to remove 5 million words from Ohio's regulatory code. In the Senate, he serves on committees including Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions; Small Business and Entrepreneurship; Environment and Public Works; and the Special Committee on Aging.

name: Richard Blumenthal
notes: Richard Blumenthal is a Democratic U.S. Senator from Connecticut who has introduced multiple AI-related bills, including the GUARD Act to ban AI companions for children and legislation to allow lawsuits over AI data training. He serves on the Senate Judiciary Committee and other committees, and was previously Connecticut's Attorney General from 1991 to 2011. Blumenthal has worked extensively on consumer protection and technology regulation issues throughout his career.

name: Jacky Rosen
notes: Jacky Rosen is a U.S. Senator from Nevada who serves as a member of the Democratic Party and was elected to the Senate in 2018, defeating Republican incumbent Dean Heller with 50.4% of the vote. She was reelected in 2024, defeating Republican nominee Sam Brown. She is described as the only former computer programmer in the U.S. Senate and has been a champion for strengthening the nation's cybersecurity. She has been ranked by independent experts as one of the most bipartisan Senators in the chamber, with more than 90% of the legislation she has introduced in the Senate being bipartisan.

name: Ted Cruz
notes: Ted Cruz serves as U.S. Senate Commerce Committee Chairman and released a legislative framework designed to promote American leadership in artificial intelligence on September 10, 2025. He introduced the SANDBOX Act, which creates a regulatory "sandbox" that gives AI developers space to test and launch new AI technologies without being held back by outdated or inflexible federal rules. Cruz is weighing a second presidential bid according to sources close to the senator, which would put him on course to collide with Vice President JD Vance. He has positioned himself as a vocal champion of traditional Republican foreign policy positions and has criticized Tucker Carlson, calling him "a coward" during a speech before a group supporting Jewish causes.

name: Michael McCaul
notes: Michael McCaul is a U.S. Congressman from Texas serving his eleventh term representing Texas' 10th District, and previously served as Chairman of the House Committee on Homeland Security and the House Foreign Affairs Committee. He announced on September 14, 2025, that he will not seek re-election in 2026. Prior to Congress, he served as Chief of Counter Terrorism and National Security in the U.S. Attorney's office, Western District of Texas, and as Texas Deputy Attorney General under current U.S. Senator John Cornyn. He has been actively involved in AI policy discussions, particularly regarding securing AI supply chains through initiatives like Pax Silica and preventing American AI technology from reaching the Chinese Communist Party.

name: Zoe Lofgren
notes: Zoe Lofgren is a Democratic member of the United States House of Representatives since 1995, representing the 18th District of California. She serves as the Ranking Member of the House Science, Space and Technology Committee and serves on the House Judiciary Committee. Lofgren chairs the 46-member California Democratic Congressional Delegation. She has been noted for her activity in tech-related policy areas such as net neutrality and digital policy, representing a district covering much of Silicon Valley.

name: Sriram Krishnan
notes: Sriram Krishnan is currently serving as the Senior White House Policy Advisor on Artificial Intelligence. He was born in Chennai, India and earned his B.Tech in Information Technology from SRM Engineering College, Anna University in Chennai. Krishnan previously worked at major tech companies including Microsoft, Facebook, Twitter, and Snap, and was a general partner at Andreessen Horowitz. He was appointed to his White House role by President-elect Donald Trump in December 2024.

name: Alondra Nelson
notes: Alondra Nelson is the Harold F. Linder Professor at the Institute for Advanced Study, where she leads the Science, Technology, and Social Values Lab. She served as deputy assistant to President Joe Biden and acting director of the White House Office of Science and Technology Policy from 2021 to 2023, becoming the first African American and first woman of color to lead OSTP. She led the development of the White House Blueprint for an AI Bill of Rights, which was a cornerstone of President Biden's Executive Order on artificial intelligence. In 2023, she was included in the inaugural TIME100 list of the most influential people in AI and was appointed by United Nations Secretary-General António Guterres to the UN High-level Advisory Body on Artificial Intelligence.

name: Mark Warner
notes: Mark Warner is a U.S. Senator from Virginia who serves as Vice Chairman of the Senate Select Committee on Intelligence. He has been leading efforts on AI policy, including introducing the Economy of the Future Commission Act with Senator Mike Rounds to prepare American workers for AI-driven workforce changes. Warner has expressed concern about AI's impact, stating 'I am terrified' about the government's preparedness for AI's potential economic disruption. He was first elected to the U.S. Senate in 2008 and previously served as Governor of Virginia from 2002 to 2006.

name: Suhas Subramanyam
notes: Suhas Subramanyam is a U.S. Representative serving Virginia's 10th Congressional District since 2025. He previously served as a technology policy advisor in the Obama White House from 2015-2017. He was the first South Asian American elected to the Virginia General Assembly in 2019. He has a background working with tech startups and co-leads AI policy initiatives in Congress.

name: Greg Abbott
notes: Greg Abbott is the 48th Governor of Texas, elected in 2014 and currently serving since January 2015. He previously served as the 50th and longest-serving Attorney General of Texas from December 2002 to January 2015, and as a Justice on the Texas Supreme Court from January 1996 to January 2001. Abbott was named one of the 100 Most Influential People in the world by TIME in 2024. He faced backlash in April 2026 for reposting an AI-generated image on X that falsely depicted the rescue of U.S. fighter jet crew members from Iran.

name: Dustin Moskovitz
notes: Dustin Moskovitz is a co-founder of Facebook and Asana, with an estimated net worth of $17.4 billion as of May 2025 according to Forbes. He co-founded the philanthropic organization Good Ventures with his wife Cari Tuna in 2011. Moskovitz and Tuna are major funders of AI safety research, having donated $1 million to Future of Life Institute and $30 million to OpenAI's non-profit in 2017. Open Philanthropy, which is financed primarily by Moskovitz and Tuna, funds AI fellows across congressional offices, federal agencies and think tanks.

name: Scott Wiener
notes: Scott Wiener is a California State Senator representing District 11, which includes San Francisco, Broadmoor, Colma, and Daly City. He authored Senate Bill 53, the Transparency in Frontier Artificial Intelligence Act, which Governor Newsom signed into law in September 2025. Wiener is running for the congressional seat held by Rep. Nancy Pelosi and has been a professional politician for going on 16 years. He previously served on the San Francisco Board of Supervisors before his election to the State Senate in 2016.

name: Yann LeCun
notes: Yann LeCun is Chief AI Scientist at Meta and Silver Professor at New York University affiliated with the NYU Center for Data Science and the Courant Institute of Mathematical Sciences. He received the 2018 ACM Turing Award shared with Geoffrey Hinton and Yoshua Bengio for contributions to deep learning. He announced plans to leave Meta after ten years to found his own company focused on world-model architectures called Advanced Machine Intelligence Labs (AMI Labs). AMI Labs raised $1.03 billion in funding at a $3.5 billion pre-money valuation in March 2026.

name: Dan Hendrycks
notes: Dan Hendrycks (born 1994 or 1995) is an American machine learning researcher who serves as the director of the Center for AI Safety, a nonprofit research organization based in San Francisco, California. He received a B.S. from the University of Chicago in 2018 and a Ph.D. from the University of California, Berkeley in Computer Science in 2022. He is the main author of the research paper that introduced the activation function GELU in 2016, and of the paper that introduced the language model benchmark MMLU (Massive Multitask Language Understanding) in 2020. Hendrycks is the safety adviser of xAI, an AI startup company founded by Elon Musk in 2023, receiving a symbolic one-dollar salary and holding no company equity.

name: Daniel Ho
notes: Daniel E. Ho is the William Benjamin Scott and Luna M. Scott Professor of Law, Professor of Political Science, Professor of Computer Science (by courtesy), Senior Fellow at Stanford's Institute for Human-Centered Artificial Intelligence, and Senior Fellow at the Stanford Institute for Economic Policy Research at Stanford University. He serves on the National Artificial Intelligence Advisory Commission (NAIAC), advising the White House on artificial intelligence, as Senior Advisor on Responsible AI at the U.S. Department of Labor, and as a Public Member of the Administrative Conference of the United States (ACUS). He is Director of the Regulation, Evaluation, and Governance Lab (RegLab) and received his J.D. from Yale Law School and Ph.D. from Harvard University. He clerked for Judge Stephen F. Williams on the U.S. Court of Appeals, District of Columbia Circuit.

name: Meredith Whittaker
notes: Meredith Whittaker is the president of the Signal Foundation and serves on its board of directors. She was formerly the Minderoo Research Professor at New York University (NYU), and chief advisor and former faculty director & co founder of the AI Now Institute. She was employed at Google for 13 years, where she founded Google's Open Research group and co-founded the M-Lab. In 2018, she was a core organizer of the Google Walkouts and resigned from the company in July 2019.

name: Bernie Sanders
notes: Bernie Sanders is an American politician serving as the senior United States senator from Vermont, a seat he has held since 2007. He is the longest-serving independent in U.S. congressional history. Sanders was reelected to a fourth Senate term in 2024, defeating Republican Gerald Malloy. He currently serves as the Ranking Member of the Senate Health, Education, Labor and Pensions Committee after previously chairing it from 2023 to 2025.

name: Alex Bores
notes: Alex Bores is an American politician serving as a member of the New York State Assembly for the 73rd district, having assumed office on January 1, 2023. He is currently running to represent New York's 12th district in the House of Representatives after announcing his candidacy in October 2025 to succeed Jerry Nadler. He co-sponsored the Responsible AI Safety and Education Act (RAISE Act), an AI safety bill that was signed into law in December 2025, and was named to Time's 2025 Time 100 AI list for his role in this legislation. His congressional campaign has been targeted by attack advertising from the AI industry-aligned super PAC Leading the Future, which has spent over $2.3 million against him by April 2026.

name: Brian Deese
notes: Brian Deese serves as Institute Innovation Fellow at Massachusetts Institute of Technology and Co-Founder and CEO at Foundry-Logic. He served as Director of the National Economic Council from 2021 to 2023 under President Biden. He previously worked as Global Head of Sustainable Investing at BlackRock and held various positions in the Obama administration including Senior Advisor to the President. He has written extensively on energy policy for AI and America's infrastructure challenges.

name: Chris Lehane
notes: Chris Lehane is Chief Global Affairs Officer at OpenAI, having previously served as vice president of global policy starting in early 2024. He is a veteran of the Clinton administration who famously coined the term "vast right-wing conspiracy" and worked during the Monica Lewinsky scandal. Before OpenAI, he served as Head of Global Policy and Public Affairs for Airbnb from 2015-2022 and was an Operating Partner at Haun Ventures, a crypto investment firm. He co-authored a book called "Masters of Disaster: The Ten Commandments of Damage Control" in 2012.

name: Erik Brynjolfsson
notes: Erik Brynjolfsson is the Jerry Yang and Akiko Yamazaki Professor and Senior Fellow at the Stanford Institute for Human-Centered AI (HAI), and Director of the Stanford Digital Economy Lab. He also is the Ralph Landau Senior Fellow at the Stanford Institute for Economic Policy Research (SIEPR), Professor by Courtesy at the Stanford Graduate School of Business and Stanford Department of Economics, and a Research Associate at the National Bureau of Economic Research (NBER). He is one of the world's leading experts on the economics of technology and artificial intelligence. Brynjolfsson has authored nine books including the bestsellers The Second Machine Age: Work, Progress and Prosperity in a Time of Brilliant Technologies, and Machine, Platform, Crowd: Harnessing Our Digital Future, and published more than 100 academic articles and five patents.

name: Tim Wu
notes: Tim Wu is the Julius Silver Professor of Law, Science and Technology at Columbia Law School. He served as Special Assistant to President Biden for competition and tech policy until 2023. Wu coined the term 'net neutrality' and is known for his work on antitrust and competition policy. He authored the book 'The Age of Extraction: How Tech Platforms Conquered the Economy and Threaten Our Future Prosperity' published in 2025.

name: Yejin Choi
notes: Yejin Choi was born in 1977 in South Korea. She is currently the Dieter Schwarz Foundation Professor of Computer Science and Senior Fellow at Stanford's Institute for Human-Centered Artificial Intelligence (HAI). She is a MacArthur Fellow and has been recognized as one of the "100 Most Influential People in AI 2025" by TIME. Her research focuses on natural language processing, commonsense reasoning, and multi-modal AI.

name: Arvind Narayanan
notes: Arvind Narayanan is a professor of computer science at Princeton University and the director of the Center for Information Technology Policy. He is a co-author of the book AI Snake Oil and the essay AI as Normal Technology, and a newsletter of the same name which is read by over 60,000 researchers, policy makers, journalists, and AI enthusiasts. He was one of TIME's inaugural list of 100 most influential people in AI and is a recipient of the Presidential Early Career Award for Scientists and Engineers (PECASE). His work was among the first to show how machine learning reflects cultural stereotypes, and his doctoral research showed the fundamental limits of de-identification.

name: Heidy Khlaaf
notes: Dr. Heidy Khlaaf is the Chief AI Scientist at the AI Now Institute focusing on the assessment and safety of AI within autonomous weapons systems and defense/national security systems. She was named to TIME's 100 Most Influential People in AI 2025 and MIT Technology Review's 35 Innovators Under 35 in 2025. She completed her Computer Science PhD at University College London in 2017, where she was advised by Nir Piterman. She is a 2008 graduate of Paxon School for Advanced Studies.

name: Randi Weingarten
notes: Randi Weingarten is president of the 1.8 million-member American Federation of Teachers (AFT), which represents teachers, paraprofessionals, higher education faculty, nurses, government employees, and early childhood educators. She was elected AFT president in 2008, after serving 11 years as president of the United Federation of Teachers in New York City. She taught history at Clara Barton High School in Brooklyn from 1991 to 1997 and holds degrees from Cornell University's School of Industrial and Labor Relations and the Cardozo School of Law. When elected AFT president in 2008, she became the first openly gay leader of an American national labor union.

name: Elliston Berry
notes: Elliston Berry was 14 years old when a classmate made and shared deepfake nude images of her, which she discovered in October 2023. She helped create an online training course to teach students, parents and school staff about non-consensual, explicit deepfake image abuse, in partnership with cybersecurity firm Adaptive Security and Pathos Consulting Group. Berry advocated for the Take It Down Act, which was signed into law by President Donald Trump and makes it a crime to share nonconsensual, explicit images. She appeared at a Capitol Hill press conference with Sen. Ted Cruz and spoke at the White House bill signing ceremony.

name: Karen Hao
notes: Karen Hao is an American journalist who currently freelances for publications like The Atlantic and co-hosts the BBC podcast The Interface. She is the author of the 2025 bestselling book 'Empire of AI: Dreams and Nightmares in Sam Altman's OpenAI' which came out May 20, 2025 and was an instant NYT bestseller. She previously worked as a reporter for The Wall Street Journal covering American and Chinese tech companies, and as a senior AI editor at MIT Technology Review. Time magazine honored her on its TIME100 AI list in 2025.

name: Mike Braun
notes: Mike Braun is the 52nd Governor of Indiana who previously served as a U.S. Senator from 2018. He is a conservative Republican who spent his career as an entrepreneur before entering politics, building a business that grew into a national company employing thousands of Americans. Braun issued an executive order creating the Indiana Initiative for Drone Dominance Task Force to support President Trump's drone dominance executive orders. He has been advocating for Indiana to become a national leader in artificial intelligence infrastructure, emphasizing the importance of data centers and energy capacity.

name: Gary Peters
notes: Gary Peters is a U.S. Senator from Michigan who has served since 2015 and serves as the Ranking Member of the Senate Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs Committee. He announced on January 28, 2025, that he will not seek reelection in 2026. Peters has been rated the most effective U.S. Senator for three consecutive Congresses by the nonpartisan Center for Effective Lawmaking. He has focused on artificial intelligence policy, introducing multiple bipartisan bills including the AI and Critical Technology Workforce Framework Act and the PREPARED for AI Act.

name: Maria Cantwell
notes: Maria Cantwell is a U.S. Senator from Washington State, serving as Ranking Member of the Senate Committee on Commerce, Science and Transportation. She has introduced multiple AI-related bills in 2026, including the NSF AI Education Act and the Small Business Artificial Intelligence Training Act. She previously served in the U.S. House of Representatives from 1993-95 and worked as vice president for consumer products at Progressive Networks (later RealNetworks). She was first elected to the U.S. Senate in 2000 and won reelection in 2024 against Republican challenger Raul Garcia.

name: Frank Lucas
notes: Frank Lucas is a Republican U.S. Representative from Oklahoma's 3rd congressional district who serves as Chairman of the House Science, Space, and Technology Committee. He introduced the Workforce for AI Trust Act (H.R. 9215) with Ranking Member Zoe Lofgren on July 30, 2024, aimed at promoting a multidisciplinary workforce to advance AI integration and training. Lucas chairs the House Financial Services Task Force on Monetary Policy, Treasury Market Resilience and Economic Prosperity and wants Congress to help stabilize financial markets by insulating the Federal Reserve from political influence. He has served in Congress since 1994 after being elected by special election to fill a vacancy.

name: Ted Lieu
notes: Ted Lieu represents California's 36th Congressional District and is serving his sixth term in Congress. He was elected by his colleagues to serve as Vice Chair of the Democratic Caucus, making him the highest ranking Asian American to have ever served in House Leadership. In the 118th Congress, Ted was the Co-Chair of the House's Bipartisan Taskforce on Artificial Intelligence. He is one of three Computer Science Majors in Congress and one of four Members of Congress with a computer science degree.

name: Ed Markey
notes: Senator Edward J. Markey (D-Mass.) is a member of the Commerce, Science and Transportation Committee and has served in the U.S. Senate since 2013. He reintroduced the Artificial Intelligence Civil Rights Act on December 2, 2025, alongside Representative Yvette Clarke, to prevent companies from using biased and discriminatory AI-powered algorithms in critical decisions affecting Americans' lives. Markey stated "We must address AI-powered bias and discrimination in the AI age" and emphasized that "Under the AI Civil Rights Act, America would show leadership in AI—not just technological leadership, but moral leadership". He is running for reelection to a third Senate term in 2026 at age 80.

name: Don Beyer
notes: Don Beyer is serving his fifth term as the U.S. Representative from Virginia's 8th District, representing Arlington, Alexandria, Falls Church, and parts of Fairfax County. He serves on Congress' Joint Economic Committee, and also serves on the House Committee on Ways and Means. He was the Lieutenant Governor of Virginia from 1990 to 1998, and was Ambassador to Switzerland and Liechtenstein under President Obama. He is currently attending George Mason University as a part time student pursuing a master's degree in machine learning, in part to help inform his work on AI in Congress.

name: Anna Eshoo
notes: Anna Eshoo is a U.S. Representative who served California's 16th Congressional District from 1993 to 2025, retiring in January 2025. She co-chairs the Congressional Artificial Intelligence (AI) Caucus and was named one of Time magazine's 100 most influential people in AI in 2023. She served as Chair of the Health Subcommittee and was the first woman in the history of Congress to hold that position. Representatives Ted Lieu, Anna Eshoo, and Ken Buck have proposed a 20-member national AI commission to develop regulatory frameworks.

name: Gavin Newsom
notes: Gavin Newsom is the 40th Governor of California who took office in 2019 and is currently serving his second term. He signed executive order N-5-26 on March 30, 2026 to regulate artificial intelligence implications prior to California government contracts, emphasizing public safety and misuse. California is home to 33 of the top 50 privately held AI companies around the globe and leads with 25% of all AI patents, conference papers, and companies. He announced California has signed agreements with four of the largest technology companies in the GenAI sector including Google, Adobe, IBM, and Microsoft to help train California's workforce for AI jobs.

name: Jay Obernolte
notes: Jay Obernolte is a U.S. Congressman representing California's 23rd District since 2021 and is the only serving member of Congress with a graduate-level degree in AI. He co-chaired the bipartisan House Task Force on AI with Ted Lieu, which produced a comprehensive report in December 2024 with 66 findings and 89 recommendations. He currently serves as Chairman of the Research and Technology Subcommittee under the House Committee on Science, Space, and Technology. Obernolte is the owner, president, and technical director of FarSight Studios, a video game development company he founded in 1990.

name: Lynne Parker
notes: Lynne Parker is Principal Deputy Director of the White House Office of Science and Technology Policy and Executive Director of the President's Council of Advisors on Science and Technology. In January 2025, she was appointed by President Donald Trump as Executive Director of the President's Council of Advisors on Science and Technology and Counselor to the Director of the White House Office of Science and Technology Policy (OSTP). She has shaped national AI policy across four US presidential administrations. Parker is Associate Vice Chancellor Emerita (retired) and Founding Director of the AI Tennessee Initiative at the University of Tennessee.

name: Arati Prabhakar
notes: Arati Prabhakar served as the 12th director of the White House Office of Science and Technology Policy from October 3, 2022 to January 20, 2025, and was the Science and Technology Advisor to President Biden. She was the first woman and person of color to be confirmed by the U.S. Senate as the Director of the White House Office of Science and Technology Policy. She was instrumental in passing President Biden's executive order on AI in 2023, which sets guidelines for tech companies to make AI safer and more transparent, and was the first to demonstrate ChatGPT to the president in the Oval Office. She previously served as the 20th Director of DARPA from 2012 to 2017 and as the 10th Director of the National Institute of Standards and Technology from 1993 to 1997, being the first woman to hold both positions.

name: Suresh Venkatasubramanian
notes: Suresh Venkatasubramanian is a Professor of Data Science and Computer Science at Brown University who directs the Center for Technological Responsibility, Reimagination and Redesign. He served as Assistant Director for Science and Justice in the White House Office of Science and Technology Policy for the 2021-2022 academic year, where he co-authored the Blueprint for an AI Bill of Rights. His research focuses on algorithmic fairness and the impact of automated decision-making systems in society. He previously held positions at the University of Utah and AT&T Labs.

name: Kathy Hochul
notes: Kathy Hochul is the 57th and first female Governor of New York State. She previously served as Erie county clerk (2007–11), U.S. representative for New York's 26th congressional district (2011–13), and lieutenant governor (2015–21) to Gov. Andrew Cuomo. In her January 13, 2026, State of the State address, Governor Hochul announced a series of proposals related to AI and technology regulation that she said are intended to protect workers and consumers. She announced the launch of the FutureWorks Commission in March 2026, which plans to offer policy proposals on AI best practices that spur innovation while protecting employees from automation.

name: Brookings Institution
notes: The Brookings Institution is an American think tank in Washington, D.C. that conducts research and education in the social sciences, primarily in economics and tax policy, metropolitan policy, governance, foreign policy, global economy, and economic development. The organization was founded in 1916 as the Institute for Government Research (IGR), with the mission of becoming "the first private organization devoted to analyzing public policy issues at the national level". Brookings states that its staff "represent diverse points of view" and describes itself as nonpartisan. The institution is currently led by President Cecilia Rouse.

name: Gray Swan
notes: Gray Swan is an AI security startup founded by Carnegie Mellon professors, based in Pittsburgh, PA. The company creates AI security solutions including Cygnal (an AI firewall) and Shade (an automated AI red teamer) to protect AI systems from attacks. Gray Swan conducted a groundbreaking study with Stanford comparing AI agents to human cybersecurity professionals in real-world penetration testing. The company runs competitive red-teaming challenges through their Arena platform, including the Ultimate Jailbreaking Championship and Agent Red-Teaming Challenge with significant prize pools.

name: Coefficient Giving (formerly Open Philanthropy)
notes: Coefficient Giving (formerly Open Philanthropy) is an American philanthropic advising and funding organization with CEO Alexander Berger. As of June 2025, Coefficient Giving has directed more than $4 billion in grants across various focus areas including global health, scientific research, pandemic preparedness, potential risks from advanced AI, and farm animal welfare. In November 2025, Open Philanthropy was renamed Coefficient Giving, reflecting its evolution from working primarily with one philanthropic partner to supporting multiple major donors pursuing cost-effective, high-impact giving. The organization's founding and most significant ongoing partnership is with Good Ventures, the foundation of Cari Tuna and Dustin Moskovitz.

name: Redwood Research
notes: Redwood Research is a nonprofit organization founded in 2021 and based in Berkeley, California, dedicated to AI safety and security research aimed at mitigating catastrophic risks from misaligned advanced AI systems. The organization focuses on developing techniques to ensure AI systems align with developers' intentions, even under adversarial conditions, with key research areas including AI control protocols and the detection of strategic deception in models. Buck Shlegeris currently serves as CEO, having previously been CTO, and Ryan Greenblatt appears to be a key researcher at the organization. The organization reported total revenues of $10,036,347 for the fiscal year ending December 2023.

name: Center on Long-Term Risk
notes: The Center on Long-Term Risk's goal is to address worst-case risks from the development and deployment of advanced AI systems, currently focused on conflict scenarios as well as technical and philosophical aspects of cooperation. The organization does interdisciplinary research, makes and recommends grants, and builds a community of professionals and other researchers around their priorities. CLR works on addressing the worst-case risks from the development and deployment of advanced AI systems in order to reduce s-risks. The organization was founded in 2013 according to external sources.

name: Center for Security and Emerging Technology (CSET)
notes: The Center for Security and Emerging Technology (CSET) is a policy research organization within Georgetown University's Walsh School of Foreign Service that was founded in 2019. When founded, it was Washington's first think tank focused entirely on security and emerging technology, and it quickly grew to be the largest research organization in the world working on AI policy. CSET provides decision-makers with data-driven analysis on the security implications of emerging technologies, currently focusing on the effects of progress in artificial intelligence (AI), advanced computing and biotechnology. The organization has received more than $100 million in funding through 2025, with Open Philanthropy as its main funder.

name: Partnership on AI
notes: Partnership on AI (PAI) is a nonprofit global community of academic, civil society, and industry organizations who come together to address the most important questions in AI. PAI is an independent, nonprofit 501(c)(3) organization and funded by charitable contributions from philanthropy and corporate entities. The organization has 141 partners from 19 countries and focuses on advancing the safety and responsibility of AI systems. PAI was originally established by a coalition of representatives from technology companies, civil society organizations, and academic institutions, and supported originally by multi-year grants from Apple, Amazon, Meta, Google/DeepMind, IBM and Microsoft.

name: Anthropic
notes: Anthropic is an artificial intelligence company founded in 2021 by former OpenAI leaders including Dario Amodei and Daniela Amodei. The company specializes in Constitutional AI and has developed Claude, a family of large language models designed for safety and enterprise use. Anthropic has experienced rapid growth, scaling from $1 billion ARR in 2024 to $14 billion in February 2026. The company is structured as a Public Benefit Corporation focused on AI safety research.

name: Founders Fund
notes: Founders Fund is an American venture capital fund formed in 2005 and based in San Francisco, with roughly $17 billion in total assets under management as of 2025. The firm is closing in on a $6 billion fundraise for its fourth growth vehicle, with this rapid-fire pace happening less than a year after it wrapped up its previous $4.6 billion fund. Founders Fund was the first institutional investor in Space Exploration Technologies (SpaceX) and Palantir Technologies, and an early investor in Facebook. The firm's investments include Airbnb, Anduril, DeepMind, Rippling, Facebook, Ramp, Palantir Technologies, Pudgy Penguins, SpaceX, Spotify, Stripe, Neuralink, and Nubank.

name: Writers Guild of America
notes: The Writers Guild of America reached a tentative four-year agreement with the Alliance of Motion Picture and Television Producers in 2026, finalized after roughly three weeks of negotiations. The deal addresses AI protections, compensation increases, and health plan strengthening, coming nearly three years after a 148-day strike in 2023. The Writers Guild of America is comprised of two different American labor unions representing writers in film, television, radio, and online media: the Writers Guild of America, East (WGAE) headquartered in New York City and affiliated with the AFL-CIO, and the Writers Guild of America West (WGAW) headquartered in Los Angeles. The WGAE was officially founded in 1954 and represents thousands of members who write content for motion pictures, television, news and online media.

name: Tarbell Center for AI Journalism
notes: The Tarbell Center for AI Journalism supports journalism that helps society navigate the emergence of increasingly advanced AI. The organization offers grants of $1,000–$20,000 to support original reporting published in established outlets, whether from freelancers or staff. The Tarbell Fellowship is a one-year program for journalists interested in covering artificial intelligence, with fellows securing a nine-month placement at a major newsroom and participating in a 10-week course covering AI and journalism fundamentals. Editorial independence is a core value for Tarbell and their grantees maintain complete autonomy over their reporting.

name: Anthropic Institute
